summary: I grew up in Los Angeles, specifically in the Boyle Heights neighborhood, which was a typical L.A. community for Japanese families like mine. My father worked as a journalist there, editing the Japanese vernacular newspaper Sangyo Nippo. We often went downtown to places like the Orpheum Theatre, and my father would buy me a panda bear from a drugstore nearby. Before the war, life in Los Angeles was a mix of American schooling and Japanese cultural education at Japanese school five days a week.

My parents were both Issei from Yamaguchi Prefecture in Japan. They met there through a fixed marriage before moving to the United States. Although I never met them in Japan, their roots from Yamaguchi shaped much of our family culture and language at home. They spoke Japanese to me since they couldn't speak English well.

My father was the editor of the Sangyo Nippo, a Japanese vernacular newspaper in Los Angeles. It mainly served local farmers in the area, and managing it showed how educated and responsible he was. This role was important in our community before the war disrupted our lives.

I was 10 years old when Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941. I remember everyone gathered around the radio listening intently, and that night we had to put up sheets to block out the light in case of air raids. At that age, I didn't fully understand the fear or the significance of the event, but those memories stuck with me.

After the evacuation, my family and I were held at the Santa Anita Assembly Center. I vividly remember the horse stables where we stayed and the grandstand where older adults made camouflage nets. The mess hall was blue, and we were fed mostly apple butter on toast, which I still remember to this day.

My oldest sister lived in Detroit with her family during the war. I didn't live there myself, but it was one of the places my siblings relocated to after we were separated. Detroit was part of the broader dispersal of our family across the country during that time.

One of my sisters lived in Buffalo, New York, where her husband worked as a Japanese translator for the International Institute. This was a unique role that helped Japanese immigrants navigate life in America during the war. Buffalo was another city where my family was spread out during those difficult years.

The International Institute in Buffalo was where my brother-in-law worked as a Japanese translator. This organization helped Japanese immigrants during the war, providing translation and support services. It was important for families like mine who were displaced and needed assistance.

After Santa Anita, my family was sent to the Rohwer War Relocation Center in Arkansas. The train ride took ten days, and I remember the long waits on side tracks and feeling like we were treated like cattle. Rohwer was very different from other camps because it was in a wooded area with lots of mosquitoes and humidity. I was active in the Girl Scouts there and attended school where our desks were made from logs.

Arkansas was where the Rohwer War Relocation Center was located, and it was very different from the desert camps because it was heavily forested and humid. We lived there during the war, and the environment was challenging with mosquitoes and dense trees all around. It was a stark contrast to my life in Los Angeles before the war.

While at Rohwer, I was part of the Girl Scouts of the USA, which was one of the few activities available for kids my age. It was a way to connect with other girls and have some normalcy despite being in a camp. Our leader was a Japanese woman who later moved to Cincinnati, and I remember her fondly.

After the war, my family moved to New Orleans because my father got involved in the shrimping industry there. This was unusual since most Japanese families didn't relocate to the South. I lived in New Orleans for 15 years and appreciated the hospitality and kindness of the people, even though segregation was still very much present.

My father entered the shrimping industry in New Orleans after the war, thanks to a Chinese man who befriended him and invited us there from Rohwer. Despite his limited English, my father was able to work in this business using his knowledge of Chinese characters. This opportunity helped our family establish a new life in the South.

Before the war, my father and I often went downtown to the Orpheum Theatre in Los Angeles to see movies. Near there, he bought me a panda bear from a drugstore, which I took with me to the camps and still have today. Those outings were special moments in my childhood before everything changed.

Living in New Orleans after the war exposed me to segregation in the South. Although Japanese people were considered white and allowed to use white restrooms, I witnessed the harsh realities of racial segregation, like blacks sitting in the back of buses and being told to move to the front. It was a different kind of discrimination than what I experienced before, and it left a strong impression on me.

In New Orleans, my father was helped by a kind Chinese American man who invited him to join the shrimping business. This friendship was crucial for our family’s survival and integration in the South. It showed me how different Asian communities could support each other during difficult times.

Louisiana, especially New Orleans, was where I lived for 15 years after the war. The culture there was very different from California; the people were more hospitable and kind, despite the ongoing segregation. It was a place where my family found a new home and where I experienced life in the segregated South firsthand.

C.D. Hoy was the Chinese man in New Orleans who befriended my father and helped him get into the shrimping industry after we left the camps. His kindness was a lifeline for our family during a difficult transition. I always remember how generous he was to us.
